heuristics.rule: Compare savings rate over 10 years, not gross salary numbers
heuristics.condition: When evaluating a US salary vs. a home country salary offer
heuristics.action: Model monthly savings after taxes, rent, and cost of living for both paths, then project net worth at 10 years
heuristics.because: A $200k US salary with $6k/month rent and 40% tax can generate less savings than a ¥30k/month salary in a Tier 2 Chinese city due to purchasing power differentials

heuristics.rule: Adjust home country salary by 1.72x purchasing power before comparing to US income
heuristics.condition: When comparing nominal salaries between US and China
heuristics.action: Multiply Chinese salary by approximately 1.72 to get equivalent purchasing power, then compare to US take-home after taxes and cost of living
heuristics.because: China is approximately 72% cheaper than the US, meaning nominal salary comparisons systematically overstate the US advantage

heuristics.rule: Treat total immigration legal costs as a direct compensation deduction
heuristics.condition: When calculating the real value of a US job offer requiring visa sponsorship
heuristics.action: Subtract $20,000–$100,000 in expected lifetime immigration legal costs from total US compensation before comparing to home country options
heuristics.because: These costs are paid directly from compensation and are invisible in standard salary comparisons, making the US offer appear more attractive than it actually is

heuristics.rule: If green card wait time exceeds 20 years, treat the US path as structurally capped
heuristics.condition: When an Indian national is evaluating long-term US career prospects
heuristics.action: Factor in 50–100+ year EB-2/EB-3 backlogs when assessing whether to stay, and quantify the opportunity cost of permanent temporary status
heuristics.because: Decades of 'temporary' status means decades of immigration tax, employer dependency, and constrained career mobility that compounding wealth projections rarely capture

heuristics.rule: Count immigration overhead as 20–100 hours per year in your time budget
heuristics.condition: When a visa-holding professional is evaluating work-life quality in the US
heuristics.action: Explicitly account for time spent on visa tracking, attorney coordination, renewal trips, and mental overhead as a recurring annual cost
heuristics.because: This cognitive and time load is real, affects work and life quality, and is never included in salary or lifestyle comparisons

heuristics.rule: Only pursue US path if targeting elite outcomes; for median outcomes, the ROI case weakens significantly
heuristics.condition: When an international student is modeling whether the $400k–$800k total investment in US education and immigration pays off
heuristics.action: Benchmark your realistic career outcome against the median, not the FAANG ceiling, before committing to the US path
heuristics.because: The investment compounds favorably for elite destinations (FAANG, top finance, top research) but often does not beat a career that started earlier at home for median outcomes

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Determines actual net worth accumulation, which is the real output of career decisions — not gross salary
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Model monthly take-home after taxes and fixed costs (rent, healthcare, transportation) for each path, project savings monthly, and compound over 10 years

decision_factors.factor: Immigration tax — financial, time, and opportunity
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Significantly reduces the real value of US compensation and restricts career mobility in ways that compound over time
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Estimate annual legal fees ($3k–$8k), total green card process costs ($20k–$100k), hours per year spent on immigration overhead, and enumerate specific career actions you cannot take on H-1B

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Determines the maximum compensation and leadership trajectory available, which drives 10–20 year wealth and impact outcomes
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess whether C-suite and VP roles are realistically accessible given language, cultural, and visa constraints in the US; assess whether returnee premium and lower leadership competition create a higher ceiling at home

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Employer knowledge of high switching costs for visa holders depresses wage growth and stunts career mobility for years
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Determine whether you are on H-1B, how many years to green card, and whether your employer knows your switching costs are high; research finding: 50% of visa-holding researchers felt trapped, 80% reported restricted job prospects

decision_factors.factor: Family and inherited wealth assets in home country
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Property, business assets, and family wealth create wealth floors unavailable abroad that dramatically alter the financial comparison
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Explicitly value family property, business interests, or inheritance expectations in the home country and include them in net worth projections

decision_factors.why_it_matters: USD has historically appreciated vs. RMB and INR, which changes long-term wealth calculations if savings are held in or converted to home currency
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Model scenarios where USD strengthens 10–30% vs. home currency over 10 years and assess how that changes relative wealth accumulation

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Home country allows free pivoting, company founding, and independent consulting without visa authorization; US H-1B actively restricts these
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask whether you can start a company, take a gap, switch industries, or consult independently in each location without immigration consequences

decision_factors.factor: Home market startup and VC ecosystem maturity
decision_factors.why_it_matters: A maturing home ecosystem means returnees can access growth opportunities, leadership roles, and equity that previously only existed in the US
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research home country VC investment rank, unicorn count, and sector competitiveness (China ranks 4th globally for VC; India 5th for unicorns); assess whether target sector is globally competitive at home

decision_factors.why_it_matters: International exposure is scarce and valued at many home country companies, creating premium positioning unavailable to those who never left
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Research whether target employers in the home country actively recruit returnees and whether international credentials command salary or role premiums

decision_factors.factor: US education and immigration total investment
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The $400k–$800k total investment must compound significantly to beat a career that started earlier at home — this threshold is often not modeled
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Sum 4-year education and living costs, immigration legal fees, and foregone earnings during study; then determine whether realistic career outcome exceeds this break-even

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in the US on H-1B for career growth
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Employer gains permanent negotiating leverage because switching costs for the visa holder are asymmetrically high, systematically depressing wage growth and career mobility for years
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Mid-career professionals who are 3–8 years into H-1B with green card pending, who have the most to lose from job gaps

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Pursuing the full US green card path
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: For Indian nationals, EB-2/EB-3 backlogs of 50–100+ years mean decades of permanent temporary status with all associated immigration taxes, effectively making the green card a theoretical endpoint rather than a real one
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Indian nationals in standard employment-based green card queues

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Comparing US vs. home country salary in nominal terms
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Systematically overstates the US advantage by ignoring purchasing power (72% cost difference with China), savings rate, effective tax burden, and immigration costs — leading to a decision based on a number that doesn't represent real wealth accumulation
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Early-career professionals making their first stay-vs-return analysis without financial modeling experience

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in the US for access to FAANG-level compensation
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: The $400k–$800k total investment in US education and immigration only generates positive ROI for elite outcomes; for median US career outcomes, a career starting earlier at home may compound more favorably
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: International students who model their financial future against best-case US outcomes rather than realistic median outcomes

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Remaining in the US for stability and established career
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Cannot start a company, consult independently, switch industries, or take career gaps without immigration consequences — forfeiting the entrepreneurial upside that home country peers can freely pursue
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Entrepreneurially inclined professionals on H-1B who delay or abandon company-founding ambitions due to visa constraints

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Including inherited or family property in informal mental accounting but not in formal financial models
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Systematically undervalues the home country option by ignoring wealth floors from family assets (property, business) that are real and material to net worth projections
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals from families with significant property or business assets in their home country

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Staying in the US as policy environment becomes less predictable
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: H-1B processing delays, reduced employer sponsorship, and visa interview slot scarcity create compounding uncertainty that affects career planning, family decisions, and mental health in ways that don't appear in compensation comparisons
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Professionals mid-process in H-1B or green card applications during periods of policy volatility

failure_patterns.pattern: Using gross nominal salary to compare US vs. home country options
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Salary numbers are the most visible and easily comparable data point; purchasing power, savings rate, and tax adjustments require deliberate modeling that most people don't do
failure_patterns.consequence: Overestimates the US financial advantage by 2–3x, leading to decisions to stay that may actually produce lower net worth over 10 years
warning_signs: Saying 'the US pays 3–4x what I'd make at home' without adjusting for cost of living
Not knowing your effective savings rate in the US vs. projected savings rate at home

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring immigration tax as a compensation component
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Legal fees and time costs are paid episodically and not visible in monthly budgets; opportunity costs of visa constraints are counterfactual and hard to quantify
failure_patterns.consequence: Underestimates true cost of US path by $20k–$100k in direct costs, plus uncounted hours and career options foregone, making stay decisions look better than they are
warning_signs: Not knowing total legal fees paid to date on immigration process
Never calculating hours per year spent on visa-related overhead

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming the glass ceiling doesn't apply to you personally
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: High performers receive positive feedback that feels like evidence of unlimited upside; cultural and structural barriers to C-suite access are invisible until you're blocked by them
failure_patterns.consequence: Professionals reach mid-career expecting VP/C-suite access that proves structurally unavailable, having already spent a decade optimizing for a ceiling they didn't know existed
warning_signs: Strong performance reviews but no expansion in scope or leadership visibility after 18+ months
No senior leaders in your organization who share your background or profile

failure_patterns.pattern: Modeling financial future against elite US outcomes rather than realistic median outcomes
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Social environments in elite universities and top companies create availability bias toward FAANG success stories; survivorship bias makes median outcomes invisible
failure_patterns.consequence: The $400k–$800k investment in US education and immigration only pays off at elite outcomes; median outcomes may be financially worse than a home country career that started earlier
warning_signs: Justifying US education costs by referencing FAANG salaries without modeling probability of achieving them
Not modeling the foregone earnings during 4+ years of US study

failure_patterns.pattern: Ignoring family and inherited wealth assets when comparing financial paths
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Family assets feel separate from 'career' decisions and are not naturally included in salary comparisons; professionals may undervalue or feel uncomfortable accounting for inherited advantages
failure_patterns.consequence: Distorts the comparison by omitting real wealth floors that make the home country path significantly more financially secure than it appears on salary alone
warning_signs: Saying 'I'd make less at home' without accounting for family property or business assets
Comparing only earned income without including net worth starting position

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating green card as a near-term milestone without modeling actual wait times
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The green card feels like a defined endpoint that justifies enduring current constraints; actual queue times for Indian nationals are not widely understood until you are in them
failure_patterns.consequence: Indian nationals discover mid-career that their wait is 50–100+ years at current rates, having already spent 10+ years in temporary status with full immigration tax burden
warning_signs: Not knowing your priority date or current USCIS cutoff date for your category
Planning major life decisions (buying property, starting a business) around an expected green card timeline that hasn't been formally modeled

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your actual monthly savings rate in the US after taxes, rent, and cost of living — and what would it be in your home city?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the US salary advantage survives cost-of-living and tax adjustment; the most common analysis failure is comparing gross salaries rather than savings rates
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If savings rate is higher in the US, model whether the gap is large enough to justify immigration tax and opportunity costs
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If savings rate is comparable or higher at home, the US financial case collapses and the decision becomes about career ceiling and lifestyle

diagnostic_questions.question: What is the total legal cost you have paid or will pay for your full immigration process, and have you subtracted this from your US compensation?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person has accounted for the $20k–$100k immigration tax that is invisible in salary comparisons
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If they have modeled it, ask whether they've also quantified the time and opportunity costs of visa constraints
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If they haven't, this is a significant blind spot — walk through the full immigration cost stack before proceeding with any financial comparison

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your realistic priority date and current USCIS cutoff for your green card category, and what does that imply about your wait time?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person understands their actual timeline to permanent status, which determines the duration of immigration tax exposure
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If wait time is 20+ years, this is a structural constraint that should reframe the entire decision — explore National Interest Waiver, O-1, or EB-1A paths
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If they don't know, they are making a major life decision without a key variable — get this information before any other analysis

diagnostic_questions.question: Are there any family assets — property, business, inheritance — in your home country that would be available to you if you returned?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is ignoring a wealth floor that materially changes the financial comparison between staying and returning
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Quantify and include these assets in the net worth comparison; they may dramatically shift the relative attractiveness of returning
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If no family assets exist, the financial comparison rests entirely on earned income and the savings rate analysis becomes more determinative

diagnostic_questions.question: What specific career actions are you currently unable to take because of your visa status?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: The true opportunity cost of the immigration tax — whether visa constraints are actively suppressing entrepreneurial, consulting, or mobility options the person would otherwise pursue
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If meaningful opportunities are being foregone, quantify their expected value and include it in the stay-vs-return comparison
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If the person says nothing is restricted, probe specifically: could you start a company today, consult independently, take a 3-month gap, or switch industries without immigration consequences?

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your target outcome a FAANG-level elite career, or are you modeling for a median professional outcome in the US?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person's financial model is based on realistic probability-weighted outcomes or best-case survivorship-biased expectations
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If targeting elite outcomes, assess probability honestly and model both the elite and median scenarios weighted by likelihood
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If targeting median outcomes, the $400k–$800k US investment likely does not beat a home country career that started earlier — this should be stated explicitly

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you researched whether returnees with your profile command a premium at employers in your home country, and in which sectors?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the returnee premium (海归 effect) applies to the person's specific background, industry, and target employers — not all returnees benefit equally
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If returnee premium exists in their sector, model the salary and leadership trajectory uplift it provides in the home country comparison
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If no premium exists in their sector, remove this factor from the home country case and rely on other variables

diagnostic_questions.question: How has the policy environment in the US changed since you began your career or education there, and how does that affect your specific visa category?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the person is making a decision based on the US immigration environment as it was when they arrived versus as it currently operates
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If they are tracking policy changes, assess whether current H-1B processing delays, reduced sponsorship, and interview slot scarcity materially change their timeline or risk
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If they are not tracking policy changes, they may be operating on outdated assumptions — update their model with current 2025 realities including 19% enrollment drop signals and employer sponsorship pullbacks

industry_realities.common_belief: The US pays 3–4x more than home countries, making it financially superior
industry_realities.actual_reality: After purchasing power adjustment, effective taxes, rent, and cost of living, the real savings rate gap is often much smaller — and in some cases, a senior role in a Tier 2 home city generates comparable or higher net savings
industry_realities.implication: Never make stay-vs-return decisions on nominal salary; always model after-tax, after-cost savings rates and project net worth over 10 years

industry_realities.common_belief: Working hard and getting strong reviews will eventually lead to C-suite or VP-level roles in the US
industry_realities.actual_reality: Many senior leadership roles remain harder to access for non-native English speakers or those without deep US cultural roots — this is a structural ceiling, not a skill gap
industry_realities.implication: Assess the leadership composition of your organization and industry before assuming the US ceiling is as high as compensation benchmarks suggest

industry_realities.common_belief: The green card is a realistic near-term milestone that justifies enduring current visa constraints
industry_realities.actual_reality: For Indian nationals in EB-2/EB-3 categories, wait times are 50–100+ years at current rates — the green card is effectively not a real planning horizon for most
industry_realities.implication: Indian nationals should treat permanent temporary status as the baseline scenario and make career and wealth decisions accordingly, rather than optimizing around a green card that may never arrive

industry_realities.common_belief: The H-1B only affects where you can work — your career mobility within that job is unaffected
industry_realities.actual_reality: Research shows 50% of visa-holding researchers felt trapped in their roles and 80% reported restricted job prospects; employer leverage from high switching costs systematically depresses wage growth
industry_realities.implication: Treat visa status as a negotiating constraint that affects salary, career pivots, and entrepreneurial options — not just geography

industry_realities.common_belief: US education investment pays off because of the salary premium it unlocks
industry_realities.actual_reality: Total investment including education, living costs, immigration fees, and foregone earnings reaches $400k–$800k before permanent status — this only generates positive ROI at elite outcomes, not median ones
industry_realities.implication: Model your realistic probability of achieving elite outcomes before committing to or continuing the US path; for median outcomes, the case is weaker than students typically model

industry_realities.common_belief: Home country markets are still far behind the US in career opportunity, especially in tech
industry_realities.actual_reality: China ranks 4th globally for VC investment with competitive AI, EV, and biotech sectors; India ranks 5th for unicorns and has a $1.25B national AI Mission; IIT Madras cohorts going to the US dropped from ~50% to 10–20% — a real opportunity signal
industry_realities.implication: Reassess home country opportunity using current data, not 10-year-old assumptions; the gap has narrowed materially and in some sectors reversed

industry_realities.common_belief: Returning home means giving up your international career advantage
industry_realities.actual_reality: Returnees (海归) have premium positioning at many home country companies that value international exposure; regulatory and language advantages are scarce and genuinely valuable in growing markets
industry_realities.implication: International credentials can be more competitively differentiating in a home market hungry for that profile than in a US market saturated with similarly credentialed immigrants

industry_realities.common_belief: Immigration legal fees are a minor administrative cost
industry_realities.actual_reality: Total immigration legal costs over a full green card process run $20,000–$100,000, paid directly from compensation and invisible in salary comparisons
industry_realities.implication: Treat immigration legal costs as a direct reduction to US compensation when building financial models

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Compares gross nominal salaries between US and home country and concludes the US pays 3–4x more
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Models after-tax take-home, monthly savings after cost of living, and 10-year net worth projections for both paths with purchasing power adjustment
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The nominal comparison systematically overstates the US advantage and leads to decisions that may produce lower actual wealth accumulation

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats visa and green card costs as background administrative expenses separate from career economics
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Includes full immigration tax — $20k–$100k in legal fees, 20–100 hours per year in overhead, and explicit enumeration of career actions blocked by visa status — as direct inputs to the financial model
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Ignoring immigration tax makes the US path appear significantly more attractive than it actually is in net compensation terms

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Extrapolates current compensation trajectory and assumes strong performance will naturally lead to senior leadership
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Researches leadership composition at senior levels, assesses structural access barriers for non-native speakers and visa holders, and separately models the ceiling in each market
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Hitting a structural ceiling mid-career after a decade of optimization is a much worse outcome than choosing the market with the higher real ceiling earlier

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats the green card as a defined near-term milestone and makes major life decisions (home purchase, business plans) around an assumed timeline
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Looks up actual priority date and USCIS cutoff date, models realistic wait time, and makes decisions based on the probability-weighted scenario of permanent temporary status
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Indian nationals who discover 50–100 year backlogs mid-career have already paid a decade of immigration tax optimizing for an outcome that was never realistic

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Uses the home country opportunity landscape as it was when they left (often 5–10 years ago) and assumes the US remains categorically superior
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Actively researches current home market data — VC rankings, unicorn counts, sector competitiveness, returnee premium — and updates priors based on current conditions
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The home market has materially improved since 2020 in China and India; decisions based on outdated mental models systematically undervalue the return option

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Models financial future against FAANG-level or elite outcomes because that is what the social environment makes salient
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Explicitly estimates probability of achieving elite vs. median outcomes and weights the financial model accordingly before committing to the US investment
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The US path only generates clearly positive ROI at elite outcomes; for median outcomes the $400k–$800k investment may not beat a home country career that started earlier

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Compares earned income streams only, ignoring family assets, inherited property, and wealth floors available in the home country
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Includes family assets, property, business interests, and inheritance expectations in net worth comparisons, recognizing these as real financial variables
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Ignoring family wealth distorts the comparison and systematically undervalues the home country option for professionals from property or business-owning families
